address: Old City, Muang Chiang Mai District

description.en:
## Overview

Chiang Mai Old City is the historic heart of northern Thailand's cultural capital, a roughly square-shaped area measuring 1,800 by 2,000 meters and enclosed by ancient walls and a wide moat. Within these centuries-old boundaries you will find more than 30 temples, tree-lined streets, traditional guesthouses, cozy cafes, and some of the best street food in the north. It is the kind of place where you can spend days wandering and still discover a hidden shrine or quiet courtyard around the next corner.

## History

The walled city was founded in 1296 by King Mengrai as the capital of the Lanna Kingdom. He chose the site at the foot of Doi Suthep mountain and constructed brick walls and a moat to protect against Burmese invasions. Although much of the original wall has crumbled over the centuries, restored sections and five historic gates remain standing, with Tha Phae Gate on the eastern side being the most iconic and best preserved. The moat still encircles the entire Old City and is particularly picturesque during the Yi Peng lantern festival in November.

## What to See

The Old City is packed with significant temples. Wat Chedi Luang houses a massive 14th-century ruined chedi that once held the Emerald Buddha. Wat Phra Singh contains the revered Phra Singh Buddha image and stunning Lanna-era murals. Wat Chiang Man, the oldest temple in the city, dates back to 1296 and shelters two ancient crystal and marble Buddha statues. Beyond the temples, explore the Three Kings Monument at the center of the Old City, stroll through quiet lanes lined with teak houses, and browse the many art galleries and boutique shops tucked into old shophouses.

## Practical Info

The Old City is free to explore at any time, though individual temples may charge small entrance fees of 20 to 40 THB. The area is compact enough to cover on foot, and renting a bicycle is a popular way to get around. The Sunday Walking Street market takes over Ratchadamnoen Road every Sunday evening and is one of the best markets in Chiang Mai. The best time to visit is from November to February when temperatures are cool and comfortable. Avoid the smoky season in March and early April when agricultural burning affects air quality.
